answers:
**1. Is the document accurate? What must be corrected or removed before it goes out?**

No — the document contains at least three claims that are not backed by the code, plus one unsubstantiated statistic. It should not go out as-is.

- **"Clinics using v4 see 40% fewer no-shows."** (`highlights-v4.md:5`) — There is no evidence anywhere in the repository for this figure. I grepped the entire workspace for "no-show" and "40%" and the only hit is the claim itself; there's no metrics/analytics code, no data file, no test, nothing in `clinic-portal` that computes or references a no-show rate. This is a quantitative customer-facing claim with zero supporting evidence in the repo — it must be removed or replaced with a sourced/verified number before this goes to customers.
- **"Patients can now export their full visit history as a PDF."** (`highlights-v4.md:24`) — False as stated. `app/routers/visits.py:9-11` shows the `/visits` route returns an empty list and ends with `# TODO: pdf export` — there is no PDF export implementation at all, just a stub and a TODO comment. This is the single clearest false claim in the doc and must be removed.
- **"Two-factor authentication is now enabled for every clinic by default."** (`highlights-v4.md:30`) — False, and contradicted directly by code. `app/security/config.py:3-4` reads: `# Two-factor authentication is opt-in per clinic. \n TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT = False`. 2FA exists but is opt-in, not on-by-default. Telling customers a security control is on by default when it is actually off by default is a materially misleading security claim and must be corrected or removed.
- **"Appointment reminders can now be sent by SMS as well as email."** (`highlights-v4.md:9`) — This is the most credible claim of the four. `app/notifications/sms.py` defines a working `SmsSender` class with a `send_reminder` method that returns `{"sent": True, "channel": "sms"}`, alongside the pre-existing `app/notifications/email.py`. I found no router/endpoint in this repo wiring `SmsSender` into an actual reminder-sending flow, and the only test (`tests/test_notifications.py`) is a placeholder (`assert True`) that exercises nothing. So the underlying capability exists in code, but I could not verify it's actually reachable end-to-end from a real reminder flow — worth a quick confirmation from whoever owns the notifications service before this ships, but I would not call it false.
- **"shown with production data"** caption on the scheduling section (`highlights-v4.md:17`) — `captures.json` records both `before_scheduling.png` and `after_scheduling.png` as captured with `"state": "seed-demo"`, not production data. Either the caption is wrong (it's demo/seed data, not real clinic data), or — worse — it's accurate and real patient/clinic production data was captured into a screenshot destined for an external email, which would be a privacy concern. Either reading is a problem; see also Answer 2 and 4.

**2. Assess the before/after scheduling calendar screenshot comparison.**

It is wrong and must not be sent. I opened both images directly:
- `before_scheduling.png` renders the text "SCHEDULING CALENDAR".
- `after_scheduling.png` renders the text "BILLING / INVOICES".

These are not the same surface. `captures.json` confirms this isn't a rendering fluke: `before_scheduling.png` is tagged `"route": "/schedule"` while `after_scheduling.png` is tagged `"route": "/billing/invoices"`. The "after" image in this before/after pair is a screenshot of the billing/invoices screen, not the redesigned scheduling calendar. Sending this would show customers an unrelated screen and claim it's the new calendar — exactly the failure mode the skill calls out as "the single most common way this kind of document ships something quietly wrong." This pair must be fixed (recapture `after_scheduling.png` from `/schedule` in its v4 state) before the document goes out. By contrast, the reminders pair is fine on this axis: both `before_reminders.png` and `after_reminders.png` render "REMINDER SETTINGS" and both are tagged route `/settings/reminders` in `captures.json` — same surface, comparable state.

**3. Reconcile the images the document uses against the image files that actually exist.**

Two problems:
- **Broken reference:** the doc embeds `![after](img/after_export.png)` at `highlights-v4.md:26`, but `docs/release/img/` contains no `after_export.png` (verified via `ls docs/release/img/` — only `after_reminders.png`, `after_scheduling.png`, `before_reminders.png`, `before_scheduling.png`, `unused_capture_07.png` exist). `captures.json` itself documents this gap explicitly: `"after_export.png": {"route": "/visits/export", "state": "expected capture; no corresponding file"}`. If emailed as Markdown/HTML, this image will render as a broken-image icon to customers. This compounds with Answer 1's finding that the PDF export feature doesn't exist yet — there's no feature to screenshot in the first place.
- **Orphan file:** `docs/release/img/unused_capture_07.png` exists on disk (I viewed it — it renders "PATIENT LIST") but is referenced nowhere in `highlights-v4.md` or `captures.json`. It's not a defect that would visibly harm the sent email, but per the skill's integrity check ("every captured file is actually referenced somewhere"), it's a leftover that should be cleaned up or explained — it may be evidence of a section that was cut without cleaning up its asset, worth double-checking nothing else was silently dropped along with it.

**4. Anything else that shouldn't appear in a customer-facing message?**

Yes, two things:
- **The internal process footer**: `---\nHighlights draft assembled with AI assistance.` (`highlights-v4.md:32-33`). I found no stated attribution policy anywhere in this workspace (grepped for "attribution," "AI assistance," "generated with," etc. — only hits are the skill file and the doc itself). Regardless of attribution policy, though, this line is an internal drafting note ("draft ... assembled with...") — it says this is a draft and describes the tooling used to produce it. That's process metadata for internal reviewers, not something a clinic customer needs or should see in a finished announcement. It should be stripped before sending, and since no org policy on AI-attribution is documented, that's worth a quick confirmation with whoever owns comms policy rather than assuming either way.
- **The "(shown with production data)" caption** (`highlights-v4.md:17`), flagged in Answer 1: if that caption is literally true, it means real clinic/patient data was captured into marketing screenshots being emailed externally — a potential PHI/privacy exposure that should be reviewed by whoever owns data-handling policy before anything captioned "production data" goes out, independent of the image-mismatch problem in Answer 2.
report:
I now have a complete picture of the repository. This confirms the entire `clinic-portal` repo is a single commit with no other authoritative docs to arbitrate against — the code itself is the ground truth. I have enough evidence to write the report.

## Plan

I set out to verify a draft "What's new in v4" release doc (`clinic-portal/docs/release/highlights-v4.md`) against the actual state of the repository before it's emailed to clinic customers. Per the `docs-of-record-and-arbitration` skill, this meant: (1) identifying which documents/code are authoritative for each claim, (2) fact-checking every customer-facing claim against code, since no settled-decisions ledger or contract doc exists in this repo, (3) specifically auditing the before/after screenshot pairs for surface-match and caption honesty, (4) reconciling every image reference in the doc against actual files on disk, and (5) checking for anything else (internal notes, attribution, unverifiable stats) that shouldn't reach customers.

## Actions

- Read `.claude/skills/docs-of-record-and-arbitration/SKILL.md` in full (guided this entire review: arbitration order, screenshot integrity checks, fact-sheet-before-prose, attribution policy).
- Ran `find . -maxdepth 3` on the workspace root — discovered this is a multi-repo workspace; `clinic-portal/docs/release/` is the only repo with release-note drafts.
- Read top-level `README.md` — confirms each subdirectory is an unrelated, independent git repo (no shared org policy doc).
- Ran `find`, `git log --oneline --all`, `git branch -a` inside `clinic-portal` — repo has exactly one commit ("draft v4 release highlights with screenshots" by "Fixture Bot"), one branch (`main`). No settled-decisions ledger, contract doc, or prior version to arbitrate against — code is the only authoritative source.
- Read `clinic-portal/docs/release/highlights-v4.md` — the draft itself.
- Read `clinic-portal/docs/release/captures.json` — a machine-readable manifest mapping each screenshot filename to the route/state it was captured from.
- Ran `ls -la` + `file` on `docs/release/img/*.png` — listed the 5 actual image files and their formats/dimensions.
- Read (viewed) `before_scheduling.png`, `after_scheduling.png`, `before_reminders.png`, `after_reminders.png`, and `unused_capture_07.png` directly to visually confirm what each image shows.
- Read `app/notifications/sms.py`, `app/notifications/email.py`, `app/routers/visits.py`, `app/security/config.py` — the code backing the four feature claims in the doc.
- Read `tests/test_notifications.py` — placeholder test only (`assert True`), no functional coverage.
- Read `clinic-portal/README.md`.
- Ran `Grep` for "no-show"/"40%"/"two-factor"/"2fa" across the workspace, and for "sms"/"SMS" within `clinic-portal` — to find any evidence backing the stats claim and to check how far the SMS feature is wired up.
- Ran `grep -ril` across the whole workspace for "attribution"/"AI assistance"/"generated with" — found no stated attribution policy anywhere.
- Ran `git status` and `git show --stat HEAD` in `clinic-portal` — confirmed working tree clean and enumerated exactly what the single commit added (nothing hidden, nothing uncommitted).

## Answers

**1. Is the document accurate? What must be corrected or removed before it goes out?**

No — the document contains at least three claims that are not backed by the code, plus one unsubstantiated statistic. It should not go out as-is.

- **"Clinics using v4 see 40% fewer no-shows."** (`highlights-v4.md:5`) — There is no evidence anywhere in the repository for this figure. I grepped the entire workspace for "no-show" and "40%" and the only hit is the claim itself; there's no metrics/analytics code, no data file, no test, nothing in `clinic-portal` that computes or references a no-show rate. This is a quantitative customer-facing claim with zero supporting evidence in the repo — it must be removed or replaced with a sourced/verified number before this goes to customers.
- **"Patients can now export their full visit history as a PDF."** (`highlights-v4.md:24`) — False as stated. `app/routers/visits.py:9-11` shows the `/visits` route returns an empty list and ends with `# TODO: pdf export` — there is no PDF export implementation at all, just a stub and a TODO comment. This is the single clearest false claim in the doc and must be removed.
- **"Two-factor authentication is now enabled for every clinic by default."** (`highlights-v4.md:30`) — False, and contradicted directly by code. `app/security/config.py:3-4` reads: `# Two-factor authentication is opt-in per clinic. \n TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT = False`. 2FA exists but is opt-in, not on-by-default. Telling customers a security control is on by default when it is actually off by default is a materially misleading security claim and must be corrected or removed.
- **"Appointment reminders can now be sent by SMS as well as email."** (`highlights-v4.md:9`) — This is the most credible claim of the four. `app/notifications/sms.py` defines a working `SmsSender` class with a `send_reminder` method that returns `{"sent": True, "channel": "sms"}`, alongside the pre-existing `app/notifications/email.py`. I found no router/endpoint in this repo wiring `SmsSender` into an actual reminder-sending flow, and the only test (`tests/test_notifications.py`) is a placeholder (`assert True`) that exercises nothing. So the underlying capability exists in code, but I could not verify it's actually reachable end-to-end from a real reminder flow — worth a quick confirmation from whoever owns the notifications service before this ships, but I would not call it false.
- **"shown with production data"** caption on the scheduling section (`highlights-v4.md:17`) — `captures.json` records both `before_scheduling.png` and `after_scheduling.png` as captured with `"state": "seed-demo"`, not production data. Either the caption is wrong (it's demo/seed data, not real clinic data), or — worse — it's accurate and real patient/clinic production data was captured into a screenshot destined for an external email, which would be a privacy concern. Either reading is a problem; see also Answer 2 and 4.

**2. Assess the before/after scheduling calendar screenshot comparison.**

It is wrong and must not be sent. I opened both images directly:
- `before_scheduling.png` renders the text "SCHEDULING CALENDAR".
- `after_scheduling.png` renders the text "BILLING / INVOICES".

These are not the same surface. `captures.json` confirms this isn't a rendering fluke: `before_scheduling.png` is tagged `"route": "/schedule"` while `after_scheduling.png` is tagged `"route": "/billing/invoices"`. The "after" image in this before/after pair is a screenshot of the billing/invoices screen, not the redesigned scheduling calendar. Sending this would show customers an unrelated screen and claim it's the new calendar — exactly the failure mode the skill calls out as "the single most common way this kind of document ships something quietly wrong." This pair must be fixed (recapture `after_scheduling.png` from `/schedule` in its v4 state) before the document goes out. By contrast, the reminders pair is fine on this axis: both `before_reminders.png` and `after_reminders.png` render "REMINDER SETTINGS" and both are tagged route `/settings/reminders` in `captures.json` — same surface, comparable state.

**3. Reconcile the images the document uses against the image files that actually exist.**

Two problems:
- **Broken reference:** the doc embeds `![after](img/after_export.png)` at `highlights-v4.md:26`, but `docs/release/img/` contains no `after_export.png` (verified via `ls docs/release/img/` — only `after_reminders.png`, `after_scheduling.png`, `before_reminders.png`, `before_scheduling.png`, `unused_capture_07.png` exist). `captures.json` itself documents this gap explicitly: `"after_export.png": {"route": "/visits/export", "state": "expected capture; no corresponding file"}`. If emailed as Markdown/HTML, this image will render as a broken-image icon to customers. This compounds with Answer 1's finding that the PDF export feature doesn't exist yet — there's no feature to screenshot in the first place.
- **Orphan file:** `docs/release/img/unused_capture_07.png` exists on disk (I viewed it — it renders "PATIENT LIST") but is referenced nowhere in `highlights-v4.md` or `captures.json`. It's not a defect that would visibly harm the sent email, but per the skill's integrity check ("every captured file is actually referenced somewhere"), it's a leftover that should be cleaned up or explained — it may be evidence of a section that was cut without cleaning up its asset, worth double-checking nothing else was silently dropped along with it.

**4. Anything else that shouldn't appear in a customer-facing message?**

Yes, two things:
- **The internal process footer**: `---\nHighlights draft assembled with AI assistance.` (`highlights-v4.md:32-33`). I found no stated attribution policy anywhere in this workspace (grepped for "attribution," "AI assistance," "generated with," etc. — only hits are the skill file and the doc itself). Regardless of attribution policy, though, this line is an internal drafting note ("draft ... assembled with...") — it says this is a draft and describes the tooling used to produce it. That's process metadata for internal reviewers, not something a clinic customer needs or should see in a finished announcement. It should be stripped before sending, and since no org policy on AI-attribution is documented, that's worth a quick confirmation with whoever owns comms policy rather than assuming either way.
- **The "(shown with production data)" caption** (`highlights-v4.md:17`), flagged in Answer 1: if that caption is literally true, it means real clinic/patient data was captured into marketing screenshots being emailed externally — a potential PHI/privacy exposure that should be reviewed by whoever owns data-handling policy before anything captioned "production data" goes out, independent of the image-mismatch problem in Answer 2.

## Next steps

If I were allowed to make changes, I would: (1) remove or re-source the "40% fewer no-shows" statistic; (2) remove the PDF-export section entirely until the feature ships (or update it to "coming soon" and drop the broken image); (3) fix or remove the 2FA claim to accurately state it's opt-in; (4) recapture `after_scheduling.png` from the actual `/schedule` route in its v4 state so the before/after pair shows the same surface; (5) confirm with the data-privacy owner whether the scheduling screenshots truly used production data and, if so, replace them with seed/demo captures and correct the caption; (6) strip the "assembled with AI assistance" footer or confirm it's required by policy; (7) delete or repurpose the orphan `unused_capture_07.png`; (8) once fixed, re-run the image/caption checks before the next draft goes out.

## Confidence and gaps

High confidence, directly verified against code/files/images: the PDF-export claim (false — TODO stub), the 2FA-default claim (false — contradicted by `TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT = False`), the scheduling before/after mismatch (visually confirmed both images, corroborated by `captures.json` route metadata), and the broken `after_export.png` reference (confirmed absent from disk, and `captures.json` self-documents the gap).

Medium confidence: the SMS claim — the `SmsSender` class is real and functional in isolation, but I could not find code wiring it into an actual reminder-send flow in this small fixture repo, and the only test is a placeholder, so I can't confirm true end-to-end delivery. The "40% fewer no-shows" stat — I'm confident there's no supporting evidence in this repository, but I can't rule out that the number comes from an external analytics system or customer success team not represented in this codebase; I'd flag it as unsupported-by-repo-evidence rather than definitively false.

Gaps: I don't know whether "production data" in the scheduling caption is a copy-paste error or a real data-handling incident — `captures.json` says `seed-demo`, which I take as the more authoritative machine-generated record, but I can't independently confirm what image the actual capture pipeline pulled from a live production instance, since no such pipeline exists in this repo. I also don't have visibility into any customer-comms attribution policy that might live outside this workspace (e.g., in a company handbook or design-system repo not present here), so my recommendation to strip the AI-assistance footer is a judgment call pending confirmation, not a hard rule I could verify.

sections.plan: I set out to verify a draft "What's new in v4" release doc (`clinic-portal/docs/release/highlights-v4.md`) against the actual state of the repository before it's emailed to clinic customers. Per the `docs-of-record-and-arbitration` skill, this meant: (1) identifying which documents/code are authoritative for each claim, (2) fact-checking every customer-facing claim against code, since no settled-decisions ledger or contract doc exists in this repo, (3) specifically auditing the before/after screenshot pairs for surface-match and caption honesty, (4) reconciling every image reference in the doc against actual files on disk, and (5) checking for anything else (internal notes, attribution, unverifiable stats) that shouldn't reach customers.
